topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Games / greedy algorithms
prompt:
In a computer network consisting of $2008$ computers no two cycles intersect. At time $t = 0$, a hacker hacks into a computer in this network; and at time $t = 1$, the network administrator installs a protective software to an unhacked computer. For each positive integer $k$, at time $t = 2k$, the hacker hacks into another computer, if there is one, that is not protected and that is directly connected to a hacked computer; and at time $t = 2k + 1$, the administrator installs the protective software to another computer, if there is one, that is not hacked and that is directly connected to a protected computer. Determine the maximum number of computers the hacker can guarantee to hack into no matter how the network is configured.

[For $m \ge 3$, $\{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m\}$ is a cycle if the computers $C_1$ and $C_m$ and, for all $2 \le i \le m$, the computers $C_{i-1}$ and $C_i$ are directly connected.]
response_text:
Let the network be a graph on \(2008\) vertices.  
The condition “no two cycles intersect” means any two cycles are vertex-disjoint. Such graphs are often called **cactus graphs**.

We want the largest number \(N\) such that **for every** such graph on \(2008\) vertices, the hacker has a strategy to eventually hack at least \(N\) computers.

## Key idea
The hacker and administrator each start from one vertex and then can only expand along edges from their own territories. So the process is like two growing connected regions.

The administrator tries to block as much as possible; the hacker wants a guaranteed lower bound.

The right invariant is that in any connected component of such a graph, the hacker can guarantee at least half the vertices, rounded up.

---

## 1. Trees
First consider a tree.

A standard fact: every tree has a **centroid** \(v\) such that every component of \(T-v\) has at most \(\frac n2\) vertices.

If the hacker starts at such a centroid, then after the administrator chooses any starting protected vertex, the tree is split into branches from \(v\). The administrator can only grow inside one branch initially, while the hacker can grow through \(v\) into all the other branches. Since each branch has size at most \(n/2\), the administrator can prevent the hacker from getting at most \(n/2\) vertices. Hence the hacker gets at least
\[
n-\left\lfloor \frac n2\right\rfloor=\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil.
\]

So for trees, the hacker can guarantee \(\lceil n/2\rceil\).

---

## 2. Cactus graphs
Now contract each cycle to a single “super-vertex”. The resulting graph is a tree. Choose a centroid block \(B\) of this block-tree.

- If \(B\) is a single vertex, we are in the tree situation.
- If \(B\) is a cycle, the hacker starts at a vertex on that cycle chosen so that the cycle is split as evenly as possible.

Because cycles are disjoint, every part attached to the cycle hangs from a unique cycle vertex. After the administrator starts somewhere, the only way for protected territory to spread into the rest of the graph is through one side of the block-tree or through one arc of the cycle. The hacker, starting from the chosen vertex of the centroid block, can expand into the complementary portions.

The centroid property of the block-tree ensures that every “hanging part” outside the chosen block has size at most \(n/2\). Also, on the cycle itself, by starting appropriately, the hacker can secure at least half of that cycle before the administrator can seal it off. Therefore the administrator cannot keep more than \(\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) vertices away from the hacker.

Hence in every cactus graph on \(n\) vertices, the hacker can guarantee at least
\[
\left\lceil \frac n2\right\rceil.
\]

So for \(n=2008\), the hacker can always guarantee at least
\[
\left\lceil \frac{2008}{2}\right\rceil=1004.
\]

---

## 3. Sharpness
We must show this is best possible.

Take the network to be a path on \(2008\) vertices.  
Whatever vertex the hacker chooses first, the administrator protects a neighboring vertex on the larger side. Thereafter the two expand linearly in opposite directions, and the hacker gets at most half the path, i.e. at most \(1004\) vertices.

So no larger guarantee is possible.

Therefore the maximum number the hacker can guarantee is

Final answer: \(1004\)
